title: Marqeta's Square Dance with Customer Concentration and Why it Might Not Matter
text: Marqeta's Customer Concentration Analysis. The Core Problem: Marqeta exhibits significant revenue dependency on a single customer. As of March 2021, 73% net revenue concentration to Square, up from 70% at year-end 2020 represented an escalating reliance on one platform provider. Why It Matters: The article highlights several risks inherent in this arrangement. Regulatory concern: Public companies must disclose any customer concentration exceeding 10% due to inherent business risks. Contract vulnerability: Marqeta's Square agreement lacks full-term lock-in protections. Negotiating power imbalance: Large customers can leverage their scale to pressure vendors on pricing. Why It Might Not Matter: Despite these concerns, the author presents a counterargument. Market precedent: Other major tech companies (Palantir, Affirm, Upstart) maintain significant concentrations yet command billion-dollar valuations. Growth opportunity: A locked $300M+ contract with Square could accelerate Marqeta's path to profitability and higher margin profile while serving as proof-of-concept for other enterprise clients. Historical resilience: Twilio survived losing its largest customer (Uber) post-IPO and now trades at substantially higher valuations. The Bottom Line: While customer concentration presents measurable risk, it hasn't materially impacted valuations for comparable companies—suggesting growth metrics may overshadow this concern in investor calculations.
